speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape four. The date is June 30th, 1995. We are interviewing Judith Goldstein, born Shapiro. My name Alice Braun. This is New Rochelle, New York, and the interview is conducted in English. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You were mentioning that you arrived arrive to this Bydgoszcz, or Torun, which is identical.

speaker: interviewee
text: At the time, I knew only the name of Torun. It was a smaller camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was like the camp, the previous camp, Stutthof? Did you see chimneys there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no, no, no. This was a much smaller camp. It was not a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this a working camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was a working camp. I mean, people did die there, out of beatings and hunger. But there was no crematory there.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the conditions-- the living conditions and the sanitary conditions, were they the same?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely the same. The food rations were the same. You got your little piece of dry bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, what was the food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, usually a square of bread-- a small square of bread, which would be in total about maybe three slices or something like that, with a tiny little square of margarine-- excuse me-- and a watery plate of soup. That was the menu for the day. And it was very difficult to go on that kind of menu. I remember at-- particularly in Torun, the SS, or the management who managed that camp, they they had a garbage place there, where they used to throw out all kinds of garbage there. And we-- I-- I think I remember, Ruthy maybe, too-- we used to go and look for food [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] where the garbage was. And I remember eating raw potato peels and imagining that I'm eating a piece of chicken. However, if they found you [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] shifting through the garbage, it was dangerous. You were at risk. You could get-- I don't know if they would shoot you, but you would get at least 25 whips. I-- I watched the horror of whips many times. I was scared to death. Hitting is to me such cruel way. I, I am totally against it. Whenever I see it on TV or, or elsewhere, I'm, I'm always horrified. It creates a terrible emotion in me. Because I remember seeing it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Ruthy was still with you, and your mom, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your aunt?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my aunt too, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you didn't work in that camp, or did you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we did, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was basically shoveling things, snow and whatever. I think this was also part of, maybe-- IAG. Maybe there were some people who were working with the same wires, you know, connecting wires for field telephones. Because I think that's why they took us out from Stutthof. Because we worked in Riga for the IAG. I think that's why they took this particular group out of Stutthof. And that saved us. And, and it was in Torun that some of us continued this kind of work.

speaker: interviewee
text: Did you can have the notion that if you work or you make yourself useful, that you'll be able to survive, or they won't kill you? Yes, we did have that notion. We had that hope, that that would keep us alive. As long as they need us, we would be alive. But they were unpredictable. I mean, you never knew with them. So you arrived to Rodun about late fall of 1944, is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: Correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this living or whatever you-- it's called-- living-- you, you lived this life in Rodun until--

speaker: interviewee
text: Torun.

speaker: interviewer
text: Torun, excuse me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It's OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Torun. And your mother worked, and you worked, and your aunt worked.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my aunt worked, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And under those conditions, which you previously described, the food and all of that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I think what also helped us is that this camp also helped us be in contact with outsiders. And occasionally my aunt used to get something from a Christian. And that did help us exist. Even though I, who lost a lot of blood-- because those nosebleeds still continued-- I was really doing very poorly, health-wise. I think maybe a piece of sugar or a piece of extra bread did help me exist.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were about 12 years at this time? Late 1944?

speaker: interviewee
text: Late, yes. Mm hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then in 1945, when you settled in this-- in this new camp, what were the conditions? Did you have an idea that the front is nearing? Or you, you didn't have a notion that the war is closing or the war, the war is coming to an end?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when we were in Torun, yes, we did get already news from outsiders that the Russians are coming closer, and that the war is already-- that the Germans are not doing as well as, as we knew in the past. We did begin, at the end, to hear the shelling and the bombs. But they were very distant. And you know, we were not allowed, or we were afraid, rather, to exhibit any kind of joyous emotion. It could have cost us our life, you know. So we had to pretend that we don't know anything. You know, because they were still around us. But within a few months in that camp, especially in the beginning of 1945. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't remember exactly when we were liberated, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] but it was during the winter, and all I know is that our eyelashes and eyebrows were white with frost. We didn't have any hair. But we-- a lot of people froze to death. Especially when they liquidated the, the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there also a liquidation of this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The liquidation of camp--

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you survive that?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, the liquidation of camp was very different from the others. The Germans decided that instead of just fleeing and running away and just leaving us to ourselves-- which would have been the better choice for us-- they will drag us along. Where, we didn't know. Snow was up to here. Frost must have been 20 Celsius or lower, which is much colder than Fahrenheit. And we were just walking. I was tagging along. And anybody who was tagging along away from the group, they would just shoot. My mother tried very hard to keep me with the group, because I had no energy. I had the nose bleeds, I was drained. And they shot a few people. If you couldn't walk together with the group, you were dead. So-- and a few people died anyway without shooting. They fell on the ground, which I saw, and later were buried in the exact same spot in the middle of the street. Because they-- the Jews didn't know what to do, so they just bury them. I remember several people myself. Their image is still very vivid in front of my eyes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say walking, what were walking in? Did you have wooden clogs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, wooden clogs, like clunk, clunk, clunk-- and very little clothes. My mother would stuff paper, you know, if we were getting some paper. I think at that point, we took our blankets from the bed. We had these like army blankets, and we wrapped ourselves around. Because the, the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] clothes that we wore were very thin. They were not winter clothes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your feet when you walked in those clogs without stockings?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know what? I don't even remember. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My, my, my husband's uncle's toes were frozen. But he was in a whole different-- so fortunately, my toes were not frozen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did they walk you to?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know. We were just walking, walking and walking. But when the shelling came closer, suddenly we realized that all the Germans-- who walked with their pistols like this and their fire guns like this-- we looked around, and they weren't there. So at that point, we were in the center of the town. Everybody ran, we all dispersed. We ran into--

speaker: interviewer
text: You happen to remember the name of that town?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was Torun.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was still in Torun?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, again, I'm still talking about Bydgoszcz. That was the same place. You see, the camp was not in the center of the town. It was outside someplace, but it was still called Torun, the camp. The center of the town was where the Germans ran away. Because the shelling was already too close. The, the Russians were coming. And-- but they, they did not occupy the town yet. So when the Germans disappeared, the Jews began to run. Actually, it was women-- we were just women. And my, my mother, my aunt, and myself, and others ran into a, a, a courtyard, you know. Again, I'm saying that the villages, and especially a smaller town, had always a courtyard with a gate. And it was a poor little town, but it was very nice. A lot of Germans lived there who ran away also.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we ran into one of those, and I don't know how we got there, but apparently we opened a door we went into a basement. And we were hiding.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we heard footsteps. And we hoped that it would be the Russians. But it wasn't. It was-- they were Germans. And my aunt, my mother, dragged me into the chimney. The chimney was on the side, not where the door was. They banged the door open. And-- and one young woman was right near the entrance, and it hit her in the leg. And the had to amputate the leg after, This was a woman, a young woman probably under 20. Had we been there, we would have either been shot or who knows. And we hid in the-- we were about five people, there were a few other people-- in the chimney, we hid. They never saw us.

speaker: interviewee
text: So and-- they didn't enter to examine the whole basement, and then they left. But by that time, I think the Christians knew. They heard some activity there, especially when the Germans came and were shooting. So by that time, the Christians knew that we are hiding there. And it didn't take long-- maybe overnight-- the Russians came. And I remember how they, they ran-- the Christians ran in to tell us that we can come out. We're free now, the Russians are here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Although, that was dangerous, too, because some Russians did not believe who we were also. It was-- I read and know, that it could have been also dangerous. Some Russians did not believe that we are--

speaker: interviewer
text: But they believed you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess so, I guess so. But they were also-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they believed that we were survivors from, from a concentration camp. And they did make us comfortable. I don't remember, really, what they did for us. But I do remember that we were invited into a home, where we were given food. And then we were escorted in the building, in an apartment, in a, in a-- not the same place, in a different courtyard-- to occupy. I think the Russians told us to occupy an empty apartment where Germans lived. And it was there where I saw the first grand piano standing. It was there that I ran over to the piano and couldn't find [SIGHS] couldn't find a tone where to begin my favorite piece. Like the keyboard was totally strange to me at that point. But it was like being in a home for the first time since home.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's four years time, from 1941 to 1945. Is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: Correct, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was not easy, too. Because my mother and my aunt tried to organize food and tried to find a means of, of-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] of income, to eat something. And I remember she would-- she once made potatoes, boiled potatoes with onions. And I literally thought I was eating chicken. I had this thing about potatoes with chicken. My mother was maybe 38 kilos. She was so thin. I don't know how thin I was. I was probably thin.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was the population at this point to you? Were they helpful to you? Did they try to help you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes they did. But oddly enough, it was dangerous for the Russians. Because they were young soldiers, they were wild. And at one day in the middle of the night, they banged on our door. And my mother came to the door and begged and begged and begged to leave us alone. She was not so much worried about herself. She was worried about my aunt and me. Mostly about my aunt, but somehow about me, too. I mean, a lot of rape went on. And they were looting a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was very difficult. Some Russians gave us food. But then at night, you didn't know what they were going to do. So it was-- it was-- we went through a hard time, even after the war. But my mother started to announce to people that she knows how to sew. And she would get little jobs here and there, and they would give her food. And it was there that we spoke to other people who were also liberated in Torun.

speaker: interviewee
text: They got some kind of communication from another city-- the city was Lodz-- that there is a much larger population of Jews there, that they are sort of congregating there, and that it might be a better idea to go where there are more Jews, with-- where you can touch base with other survivors and register [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as a survivor.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did you leave there? After maybe a few weeks, or a few months?

speaker: interviewee
text: A few months I think, a few months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you went down to Lodz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened in Lodz?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] My mother found a little place. Again, we shared it with other survivors. It was one apartment. There were several families in one apartment, but at least you had some privacy. And there, it was there where she made a lot of contacts. She used to go every single day to the Judenrat, like to an office, a central office, to see if maybe my brother or my father showed up. My father said that if we do survive the war, we should really returned to Vilna, because this would be our meeting place. We never did. Not because we didn't listen to him. It's just that we begin to hear stories about him, and we didn't think that he survived. There were some people who actually saw him and then didn't see him again. So we didn't believe that he is alive. And he-- he is not. He was not alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: And about your brother, you didn't find out anything at that point.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not yet, not yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: As a matter of fact, we met a lot of people there in Lodz. And my mother once heard that people go even to the trains, and they watch passing trains on the train station. So she went to the station, and she was standing there, just looking for trains. But she never met him. But my brother did pass Lodz. He was liberated in Buchenwald-- or maybe, I don't know, I really shouldn't tell his story. He was in Buchenwald. He was found among the dead. He was sick with typhus, and he was in the hospital for many, many months until he recovered. And when he recovered, he was about to go to Vilna. So he passed Lodz. And somehow when the train stopped and saw other people there, somebody recognized him and said to him, Meir, get off this train. Your mother and sister are alive. And it was a very emotional moment for him. He later told me that he could have-- he dealt with the death of his parents more. What he really had a hard time dealing with, his little sister dying. That he had-- and he knew for sure that I was not alive, because he also knew that they killed children. Not that he was old, but he was you know, older.

speaker: interviewee
text: So at that point, he did go to the center there. And my mother was informed that he's alive. My mother was very strict with me after the war, meaning that she wanted me home at a certain time. She didn't-- past 9 o'clock, I couldn't be anywhere else, I had to be home. She was very protective, and, and-- was worried. She was always worried about me. I shouldn't get lost, I shouldn't do this or that. And I remember coming in one day, maybe I was playing with other kids in the yard. And came in, it was maybe five after 9:00. And she says, come here, come here. Didn't I tell you to be home at 9 o'clock?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I thought, oh my God, she's going to punish me or something. And she leads me into the kitchen. and my brother stands there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you recognize him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we fell into an embrace. It's easier for me to tell the story now, because I've told it before. I think our embrace was about 20 minutes, we never saw each other. We never looked at each other, we were just holding each other tight. Because I never thought that I would ever see him again. And he never thought that he would see me again. So we did become a partial family at that point.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you stayed on in Lodz until 1946, about? Or you left soon--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yes, I-- yes, I think we did, yes. It was probably-- actually, I even went to a school there. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, you continued your education?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my mother was a great believer in education. I don't think my brother went, but I did. My brother actually, at that point-- from the ghetto, actually-- knew Hebrew. And he--he was much more educated than I was. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: But he was older, wasn't he?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was older, right. So I did-- she enrolled me in a, in a-- like a public school. I don't even remember if it was a real public school, or if it was like a created public school for Jewish children. Because I don't remember going to school with other--with other children who were not of my faith. And again, there already, immediately I participated in, in dancing and singing and plays. And I was already into these things. But I still did not play the piano. I still did not take lessons. It was not available, and it was just not-- it was-- there was no--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think you would have been able to play, even if--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I didn't know if I was ever going to play the piano. I wanted it desperately.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then I found a neighbor who did have a piano. And I started to acquaint myself again with the keyboard. But I really didn't take any lessons, until I got to Germany. That was when I resumed. So your next step was going to Germany. Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, that wasn't easy. Because we belonged-- my brother joined a group of people who were sort of like a kibbutz, people who were getting ready to go to Israel. Because that was the ultimate goal for us. During the war, I said that if and when I survived the war, I will-- if I grow up, I will not continue to be Jewish again. I will not marry Jewish, I will not have Jewish children. I didn't want to be Jewish anymore. This was me speaking as a child.

speaker: interviewee
text: I resented the whole scenario. And then I said, well what for? Why should I be Jewish? So that my children can go through what I went through? So that I-- my life will be in constant danger? I was sick and tired of anti-Semitism and prosecutions.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the idea of Israel was introduced to me there. And all the kibbutzniks-- you know, not the real kibbutz like in, in, in Israel. And the whole thing attracted me again.

speaker: interviewee
text: So here I was Jewish again. And I took a great interest in the language and, and in Hebrew. Well, my brother spoke fluently Hebrew already. I didn't though, but I acquainted myself with some Hebrew words. It wasn't until later that I learned more Hebrew. So the kibbutz arranged transports to cross from Poland to Germany. Because we knew-- that is, my mother knew, my brother knew-- that if we stay in Poland, we will be trapped by the Russians. We will never get out. And it just happened so, Lodz was under the Russian jurisdiction. So we got ready to go to Germany. And that wasn't easy either, because the, the kibbutz made special arrangements. And one day we were supposed to go on a transport, and I got sick. I had high fever, and we were very upset that we couldn't go. Well, you know what happened to that whole transport? They caught them in the mountains. Poles, who were still very much into the, the scheme of anti-Semitism, caught the entire group and shoot them down like sheep, right on the border.

speaker: interviewee
text: And again, one more time, I escaped. Not only I-- my mother, my brother. Not my aunt. My aunt, I think, came later.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that was one more time. There were many incidents. It's really a miracle. I think I believe in miracles. So where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I got sick, and we didn't go on that transport. I still have pictures of certain youngsters who were on that transport and and were shot. So.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your friend Ruthy with you at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. I had lost contact with her. I didn't see--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was that? In Torun?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I think after that we were really separated. So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't meet Ruthy again, until in the United States, you know. So-- [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you join another transport towards Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we did. And we were reassured that it will be guarded differently, better, and that we would be safer. And we did cross into the German zone. We passed through Vienna, we wound up in [SIGHS] Dalsheim.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that, Dalsheim?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was near Frankfurt-- Frankfurt am Mein?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, what was the Dalsheim?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a displaced persons camp, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you registered there, or what was the purpose? Were you still ready to go to Israel, or what happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, at that time we were not-- nothing was ready yet. Israel was not in existence yet. We wound up in Dalsheim, which was a displaced persons camp. And it was on the American zone. And the Americans treated us nicely, where we had enough food. We had-- we lived, actually, in a big, big villa. They had two, two pools there. They weren't in use, but it was like a German officers kind of a place, where the conditions were pretty good. But we--we had like little rooms, like-- it wasn't a barrack anymore. You had a regular bed. And you had some linen. And we ate together. We ate in a, in a joint kind of a dining room. And the food was OK. In fact, Ben-Gurion came to visit us, yes. And I played 'Hatikvah' on the piano. At that point, I knew already how to play a little bit more. Yes, and that was very moving. My brother stood next to him. I don't have a photograph, but my brother has a photograph standing next to Ben-Gurion, holding the, the flag. But that was not until after, when Israel was a, a land already, it it was our land. Basically, at that kibbutz-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] again, it involved work just to run the kibbutz. People were working outside of the kibbutz. They created in that particular lager-- it was a nice lager-- a displaced persons camp. They had a school, and I-- that's where I, I really joined the academic road, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what, what were you-- what was the language spoken in school? What, what did you-- what language did you learn in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was a problem. [LAUGHS] We were a mix. We were a mixed bag. A lot of us, a lot of kids from Poland knew Polish very well. I didn't. I knew Yiddish very well. I couldn't-- so this was really-- I knew German. Not very well, but I knew German. I certainly could, could communicate pretty well in German.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had to kind of divide our-- our educational approach. Because some of the teachers were Jews, and some of them were not. Most of them were Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had English teachers, we had Latin teachers, we had math teachers, history teachers. These were all intellectuals and educators that survived the war and were actually working in the camp, to work with children. And we were-- I was not in one class. We were all together, younger and older. I had people five, six years older than myself. My brother was in the same. Of course, it was easier for him. He knew more. And for other kids, it was easier. But my mother took a tutor, especially for English, for Latin, and arithmetic and-- so that I could sort of, sort of catch up with the rest of them. There was no such thing as a class. And it was a good experience. It was also there that I enrolled in a conservatory of music.

speaker: interviewer
text: You started again on your music.
